answer:
**Charles Dunoyer's conception of *la liberté* (liberty) is fundamentally unitary: it denotes the *pouvoir* (power/capacity) acquired by an agent—whether an individual or a collective entity like an industry—to exercise its forces (*forces*) or faculties (*facultés*) more easily by progressively freeing itself (*s'affranchir*) from internal and external obstacles.**[^1]

This definition is elaborated in Vol. I, Ch. 1, where Dunoyer explicitly rejects common misconceptions (e.g., liberty as mere license or a pre-social absolute) and roots it in the natural progress of human development: "Ce que j'appelle *liberté*, dans ce livre, c'est ce *pouvoir* que l'homme acquiert d'user de ses forces plus facilement *à mesure qu'il s'affranchit* des obstacles qui en gênaient originairement l'exercice."[^2] Here, liberty is an *individual capacity*: it grows through self-mastery (overcoming ignorance, passions, vices) and moral habits that expand one's sphere of effective action without harming self or others.[^3] It is relative, measurable by civilization's advance ("la vraie mesure de la liberté c'est la civilisation"), and incompatible with violence or injustice, which contract one's power.[^4]

Dunoyer extends this seamlessly to *industries* as collective agents in Vol. II. In Ch. 14 ("Des conditions auxquelles toute industrie peut être libre"), industry (*industrie*)—encompassing all productive functions—is "free" (*libre*) when unhindered by monopolies, regulations, or prohibitions that obstruct its forces (e.g., division of labor, machinery, competition).[^5] These are "obstacles" analogous to personal vices: guilds, prohibitions, and state interventions limit industry's power, just as ignorance limits the individual.[^6] Liberty for industry means conditions allowing full deployment of its means (*moyens*), yielding greater productivity and societal wealth.[^7]

Ch. 16 applies this to manufacturing (*industrie manufacturière*): its "liberté" requires removing historical barriers (e.g., jurandes, regulations on tools/processes) that prevented agglomeration of workers, machinery, and markets.[^8] Collective liberty here is industry's capacity to transform matter freely, mirroring individual liberty but at scale—e.g., free competition supplanting rivals via superior *use of forces* (*meilleur usage de ses forces*), not violence.[^9] Dunoyer critiques how regulations treat industry as servile, denying it the "latitude" (*latitude*) individuals gain through moral progress.[^10]

**No strict dichotomy exists**: 'La liberté' is always *capacity qua freedom from obstacles*, scaling from personal (*pouvoir d'user de ses forces*) to systemic (industry's *libre exercice* via moral, regulatory emancipation). Industry's liberty presupposes individual virtues (e.g., prudence, activity) and vice versa; both culminate in a "perfected society" where forces align harmoniously.[^11]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Ch. 1 ("Ce que l'auteur entend par le mot liberté"), [translation/#p-fc8999](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-fc8999).
[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Ch. 1, [original/#p-8a44a6](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/original/#p-8a44a6).
[^3]: Ibid., [translation/#p-ca2a56](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-ca2a56) (development); [translation/#p-ec06ba](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-ec06ba) (self-restraint).
[^4]: Ibid., [translation/#p-39cc44](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-39cc44); [translation/#p-e80d8f](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-e80d8f).
[^5]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, Ch. 14 ("IV. Des conditions auxquelles toute industrie peut être libre"), [translation/#p-a3886e](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/14-iv-des-conditions-auxquelles-toute-industrie-peut-/translation/#p-a3886e).
[^6]: Ibid., [original/#p-803317](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/14-iv-des-conditions-auxquelles-toute-industrie-peut-/original/#p-803317) (personal maintenance as productive).
[^7]: Ibid., [translation/#p-dd5816](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/14-iv-des-conditions-auxquelles-toute-industrie-peut-/translation/#p-dd5816).
[^8]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, Ch. 16 ("VI. De la liberté de l'industrie manufacturière"), [translation/#p-4b6a43](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-4b6a43).
[^9]: Ibid., [translation/#p-ce0522](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-ce0522).
[^10]: Ibid., [translation/#p-32870d](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-32870d).
[^11]: *VOL I*, Ch. 1 [translation/#p-709973](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-709973); *VOL II*, Ch. 14 [translation/#p-13774e](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/14-iv-des-conditions-auxquelles-toute-industrie-peut-/translation/#p-13774e).
